stem: Consider \( f(x) = x^{3} + [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]x + [[C]] \) and the prime \( p = [[p]] \). Eisenstein's criterion says \( f \) is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Q} \) if \( p \) divides every coefficient EXCEPT the leading one, and \( p^{2} \) does NOT divide the constant term. Does Eisenstein's criterion at \( p = [[p]] \) confirm that \( f(x) \) is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Q} \)? Enter \( 1 \) for yes, \( 0 \) for no.
solution: Check each condition at \( p=[[p]] \): \( p \mid [[A]] \)? \( p \mid [[B]] \)? \( p \mid [[C]] \)? and \( p^{2} \nmid [[C]] \)? All four must hold for Eisenstein to apply. Evaluating these conditions directly gives the verdict \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flipped verdict, swapping whether Eisenstein applies or not.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} + [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]x + [[C]] \), a monic polynomial with integer coefficients. By the Rational Root Theorem, any integer root of \( f \) must divide the constant term \( [[C]] \), so it must lie between \( -|[[C]]| \) and \( |[[C]]| \). How many integers \( k \) with \( -|[[C]]| \le k \le |[[C]]| \) satisfy \( f(k) = 0 \)? (If this count is \( 0 \), \( f \) has no linear factor over \( \mathbb{Q} \).)
solution: Testing every candidate \( k \) from \( -|[[C]]| \) to \( |[[C]]| \) against \( f(k) = k^{3} + [[A]]k^{2} + [[B]]k + [[C]] \) gives exactly \( [[answer]] \) integer roots. A cubic is reducible over \( \mathbb{Q} \) precisely when it has a rational (here, integer, since it is monic) root, so a count of \( 0 \) certifies irreducibility over \( \mathbb{Q} \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes \( f \) has no roots at all without testing every candidate divisor of the constant term.
This assumes exactly two roots without testing every candidate.
This assumes exactly three roots without testing every candidate.
This exceeds the maximum of 3 roots a cubic can have, and is not obtained by testing candidates.
This exceeds the maximum of 3 roots a cubic can have, and is not obtained by testing candidates.
This is the search bound itself, not the number of candidates that actually satisfy \( f(k)=0 \).
This overcounts the true root count by three.

stem: Consider \( f(x) = x^{3} + [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]x + [[C]] \) and the five candidate primes \( [[p1]], [[p2]], [[p3]], [[p4]], [[p5]] \). For how many of these five primes \( p \) does Eisenstein's criterion apply to \( f \) (that is, \( p \) divides \( [[A]] \), \( [[B]] \), and \( [[C]] \), but \( p^{2} \) does not divide \( [[C]] \))?
solution: Checking each of the five primes against the four Eisenstein conditions (divides \( [[A]] \), divides \( [[B]] \), divides \( [[C]] \), and \( p^{2} \) does not divide \( [[C]] \)) shows exactly \( [[answer]] \) of them make the criterion apply.
distractor_rationales: This assumes every one of the five candidate primes works, without checking the divisibility conditions.
This overcounts the true number of qualifying primes by one.
This overcounts the true number of qualifying primes by two.
This assumes exactly one candidate prime works without checking every one.
This assumes exactly two candidate primes work without checking every one.
This assumes all but one candidate prime works without checking every one.
